items.title: Estimating coin bias from 8 flips
items.scenario: You observe H, H, T, H, H, H, T, H (6 heads, 2 tails) and model each flip as Bernoulli(p). The likelihood is L(p) = p⁶(1−p)². Maximizing ℓ(p) = 6 log p + 2 log(1−p) gives p̂ = 6/8 = 0.75.
items.explanation: Bernoulli MLE is the sample proportion of successes—the parameter that makes the observed sequence most probable.

items.title: Gaussian mean from exam scores
items.scenario: Ten scores {72, 85, 90, 78, 88, 91, 80, 84, 87, 75} are modeled as i.i.d. N(μ, σ²) with σ² known. Maximizing ℓ(μ) = −(1/2σ²)Σ(xᵢ−μ)² yields μ̂ = x̄ = 83.0.
items.explanation: With fixed variance, the Gaussian log-likelihood is maximized at the sample mean—linking MLE to averaging data.

items.title: Training a spam filter with log-loss
items.scenario: A logistic model outputs P(y=1|x, θ). On 50,000 labeled emails, the team minimizes average −log P(yᵢ|xᵢ, θ) using mini-batch gradient descent. Each step increases the log-likelihood of observed labels under the Bernoulli model.
items.explanation: No closed-form θ exists for rich features; numerical MLE via NLL minimization is standard in production classifiers.

items.title: Boundary MLE with all failures
items.scenario: In a clinical trial with 0 successes in 12 patients, Bernoulli MLE gives p̂ = 0/12 = 0. The estimate sits on the boundary of the parameter space (p ∈ [0,1]), and uncertainty is high despite the formal MLE.
items.explanation: MLE can hit edges of the parameter space on small or extreme samples—motivating smoothing, priors, or larger datasets.
